local_names.romanized_local: Ding Xiang (Chinese); Cengkih (Malay); Kirambu (Tamil); Laung (Hindi); Cengkeh (Indonesian)

cuisine: Global spice — widely used across South Asian, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, and European cuisines

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of dried cloves is approximately 1–2 g (about 1 teaspoon of whole buds), used as a flavouring agent in cooking, baking, or spice blends.

nutrient_source.title: Cloves, ground — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170416); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (F10060018)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are taken from the Singapore HPB Foodo record F10060018 (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient detail are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cloves (FDC ID 170416), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for dried whole clove buds.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices — analogous estimate; cloves have negligible digestible starch and very high fibre (33.9 g/100g), consistent with low-GI dried spice category per Atkinson et al. 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried whole cloves exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Cloves are used in culinary quantities of 1-2 g per serving; their carbohydrate content is predominantly non-digestible fibre (33.9 g/100g dietary fibre vs 31.6 g/100g total carbohydrate). Net digestible carbohydrate is negligible at culinary doses. A GI of approximately 10 is assigned by analogy with other dried aromatic spices with very high fibre and negligible starch, consistent with the low-GI classification in Atkinson et al. 2008. Glycemic load per serving = (10 x 0.63 g digestible carb per 2 g serving) / 100 = 0.06.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium contribution at culinary doses; high potassium (1020 mg/100g) supports vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Although dried cloves contain 277 mg sodium per 100 g, a typical culinary serving of 1-2 g contributes only 2.8-5.5 mg sodium, which is clinically negligible; the exceptionally high potassium content (1020 mg/100g) and eugenol-mediated vasodilatory effects make cloves a net-positive spice for blood pressure management when used in standard culinary amounts.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low sugar (2.4 g/100g), very high dietary fibre (33.9 g/100g), and eugenol polyphenols with evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried cloves have a very low glycemic index (~10) and contribute negligible digestible carbohydrate at culinary serving sizes of 1-2 g; their polyphenol content, particularly eugenol and flavonoids, has demonstrated insulin-sensitising and postprandial glucose-attenuating effects in clinical studies, making cloves a beneficial flavouring choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory eugenol, high potassium, high dietary fibre, and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried cloves are cholesterol-free and contain eugenol, a potent COX-2 inhibitor with anti-platelet and anti-inflammatory properties that may reduce cardiovascular risk markers; their high fibre content (33.9 g/100g) and potassium (1020 mg/100g) further support lipid management and vascular health, and their saturated fat content (~3.5 g/100g) is low relative to total fat, making them a cardioprotective spice at standard culinary doses.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eugenol inhibits COX-2 inflammatory pathways; low saturated fat; no trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cloves are rich in eugenol, a potent anti-inflammatory polyphenol that inhibits COX-2 pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, consumption exceeding 5 g/day may interact with anticoagulant medications commonly co-prescribed with RA biologics, so use should remain within standard culinary amounts.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried clove (Syzygium aromaticum) consists of the dried, unopened flower buds of the clove tree, native to the Maluku Islands (Spice Islands) of Indonesia. The buds are dark brown, nail-shaped, and firm in texture, with an intensely aromatic, warm, slightly sweet, and pungent flavour profile dominated by eugenol, the primary bioactive volatile compound. Cloves are one of the world's oldest and most traded spices, historically central to the spice trade and now used globally across South Asian, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, African, and European culinary traditions. In cooking, dried cloves are used whole or ground as a flavouring in rice dishes (biryani, nasi lemak), curries, stews, marinades, mulled wines, baked goods, and spice blends such as garam masala, five-spice powder, and ras el hanout. Nutritionally, dried cloves are exceptionally rich in dietary fibre (33.9 g/100g), potassium (1020 mg/100g), calcium (632 mg/100g), and iron (11.83 mg/100g), with moderate protein (6.0 g/100g) and fat (13.0 g/100g). Their energy density is 332 kcal/100g. Clinically, eugenol provides anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antimicrobial, and hypoglycaemic properties supported by peer-reviewed evidence. Cloves are suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when used in standard culinary quantities of 1-2 g per serving. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: What are dried cloves used for in cooking?
How much sodium is in cloves?
What is the glycemic index of cloves?
Can I eat cloves if I have high blood pressure?
What nutrients are in dried cloves?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database record F10060018 with confidence 0.95. Micronutrient detail cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170416 for dried cloves. Both are approved primary sources. GI is an analogous estimate documented per protocol.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170416) — cloves contain no trans fatty acids. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — plant-derived spice contains no vitamin B12 or vitamin D. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F10060018 and USDA FDC 170416 — plant-derived food contains no cholesterol. GI value of 10 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for dried whole cloves exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Dietary tag 'wholegrain' from input has been removed as cloves are a flower bud spice, not a grain product. Serving size set at 2 g reflecting standard culinary use.
